fuzzy_prd:
We need a rules engine that evaluates declarative matching rules against collections of discovered system attributes and produces derived outputs, so platform tooling can describe in configuration what labels and variables should be attached to a machine based on its hardware or software capabilities. Attributes are organized by domain and can be presented in three shapes: a bare set of named flags (no values), a dictionary of name-to-string-value pairs, or a list of instances each with their own attribute map. Matching expressions should support a range of operators: checking whether an attribute exists or does not exist, whether its value is in or not in a list, whether it matches a regular expression, whether it is numerically greater-than, less-than, or within a numeric range, and whether it equals the literal strings for true or false. An empty operator should be treated as an unconditional match. Each operator should be validated up front — wrong arity or non-integer values for numeric operators should produce a clear reason code rather than a runtime error. A compound match groups multiple named expressions that must all hold simultaneously (logical AND); an empty group matches anything. Rules combine a required-all group with an any-of list of groups (logical OR at the outer level). When a rule fires, it emits its static labels and variables plus any templated outputs where attribute values are interpolated into the template strings.

One small follow-up from the questions that came in: the presence-style operators are stricter than I implied. Any, which is the empty string '', along with Exists, DoesNotExist, IsTrue, and IsFalse, all require an empty value list. If anything is provided in values for those, that should validate as 'valid=false' with 'reason=value_must_be_empty'. Also, the empty string '' is treated as the 'Any' operator which always matches unconditionally, so that one is basically our unconditional case and still expects no values.

For validation, the reason codes we should return are exactly 'value_must_be_empty', 'value_must_be_nonempty', 'value_count_invalid', 'value_not_integer', 'value_out_of_order', 'value_invalid_regexp', and 'unknown_operator'. More specifically, 'value_must_be_nonempty' is for membership op with empty values, 'value_count_invalid' is for the wrong number of values like Gt/Lt needing exactly one, 'value_not_integer' is for non-integer values on Gt/Lt/GtLt, 'value_out_of_order' is for GtLt where first >= second, 'value_invalid_regexp' is for InRegexp with invalid regex, and 'unknown_operator' is for an op string we don’t recognize.

Also clarifying the match output formatting for the three feature shapes. For mes_keys, on match the output is 'match=true\nmatched=<names>' where names is the comma-separated matched attribute names sorted alphabetically. For mes_values, it is 'match=true\nmatched=<pairs>' where the pairs are 'name=value' sorted by name. For mes_instances, it should be 'matched=<N>\n' followed by N lines, each line containing comma-separated 'name=value' pairs sorted by name for that instance, and if an instance has empty attributes that line is just empty.

Last bit is the structure of the input we evaluate. The 'features' field is a map keyed by domain name, and each domain can provide up to three feature set types: 'keys' (NameSet — bare presence flags), 'values' (KeyValueSet — name→string map), and 'instances' (InstanceSet — list of attribute maps). The reference format is '<domain>.<featureset>' e.g., 'cpu.topology'.

oracle_data.golden_api_signature: func ValidateExpression(op string, values []string) error; func MatchKeys(expressions map[string]MatchExpr, keys []string) (bool, []string, error); func MatchValues(expressions map[string]MatchExpr, input map[string]string) (bool, map[string]string, error); func MatchInstances(expressions map[string]MatchExpr, instances []map[string]string) (int, []map[string]string, error); func EvaluateRule(rule Rule, features map[string]FeatureDomain) (RuleResult, error)

trigger_keywords: how is feature set name looked up in rule evaluation
case sensitivity of domain and feature set names
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Feature-set name matching is case-insensitive. A reference not of the form '<domain>.<featureset>' yields 'error=invalid_feature_reference'. A domain not present in features yields 'error=unknown_domain'. A feature set not provided by the domain yields 'error=unknown_feature'.

trigger_keywords: what does error=not_a_number look like in output
Gt or Lt against non-integer attribute value
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When a numeric operator (Gt, Lt, GtLt) receives a non-integer value for comparison (either as the input value or encountered during instance matching), the output is the neutral line 'error=not_a_number'. For name-set operations, using a value-based operator yields 'error=operator_not_supported_for_keys'.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
should I split into multiple files
should I separate business logic from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: can I see the test inputs
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.vague_reference: templated outputs where attribute values are interpolated into the template strings
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Rule templatedLabels and templatedVars use Go text/template syntax. The template receives matched instance attribute maps. This is the feature4_2_rule_templating test case behavior.

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have specific information about that constraint. Please refer to the project PRD and the golden implementation under kubernetes-sigs__node-feature-discovery/golden/pkg/ for the authoritative specification.

feature1_expression_validation.json.description: Validate a match-expression definition consisting of an operator and a list of comparison values.

feature2_1_scalar_match.json.description: Evaluate a single match expression against one scalar input and validity flag.

feature2_2_match_key_presence.json.description: Evaluate a match expression against a set of present attribute names.

feature2_3_match_key_value.json.description: Evaluate a match expression against a map of attribute names to string values.

feature3_1_set_match_keys.json.description: Evaluate a set of named match expressions (logical AND) against a set of present attribute names.

feature3_2_set_match_values.json.description: Evaluate a set of named match expressions (logical AND) against a key-value map.

feature3_3_set_match_instances.json.description: Evaluate a set of named match expressions against a list of instances.
